speaker: interviewer
text: OK, This is tape 4. We're with Regina-- let me say it correctly. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Weber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina Weber. This is the 10th of March, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina, we were now in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're living with your father, the three girls. It's not good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the three girls, because my sister [NON-ENGLISH] got married in two weeks. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Two weeks, and now it's two girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two girls, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] is married, Berta-- that's Berta--

speaker: interviewee
text: Berta is married.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's married. And the two girls-- and what happened then? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- Izabel certainly tried. I didn't try as much as Izabel.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get along with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: To get along my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very angry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very angry because he kept on telling me, you killed your mother. And I said, if anybody, you killed our mother. And I don't feel that way anymore. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you think killed you mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] The Germans-- nothing to do really with my father. He didn't want this to happen. No human being wanted this to happen. And I'm sorry it happened to him. But I guess we-- the girls and my brother-- had to save our lives. And he threw us out. He couldn't take us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was 17 and 1/2. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: --always said-- we had-- my brother was here already. And he took one suitcase for the three of us. And we never knew where are we going to sleep at night because the US Army was coming home. And we couldn't even get a hotel room. But then it-- I went to work. And I didn't know where I'm going to go for dinner, or where-- we ate in the hotel room some sandwiches, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister was working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my sister wasn't, just-- my brother was working, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on 57th Street in a very fancy couturier house. And I was in the sewing department. I did some interesting things. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anyway, with Phillip's-- what Phillip made and I made together, we were just about able to very mildly support ourselves and feel guilty about our father--

speaker: interviewer
text: You did?

speaker: interviewee
text: --feel that we-- that we left him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did feel guilty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, felt for years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were living in hotels?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yes, there was no apartment built or available in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] You won't believe it, because now it's really just-- if it's there, it's a whorehouse, the Hotel Dixie on, on 42nd Street. But we managed. We only stayed-- they-- you could only stay in a hotel for four days because they had to change the people. Anyway, eventually, Izabel met a Hungarian psychologist, a male, who wanted our story in But we decided against it because we said, that's going to just end up with smelly fish the next today. We don't want anything like that. And he found us a hotel-- Chelsea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very famous hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the owner was Hungarian. And he was Hungarian. And we lived there for about a year or two. And then we started to meet people, very interesting Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started fluently to speak English in six months. Because that's what we wanted to be, Americans, you know? We loved America. It was just to made to order for us. We were worldly--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were on 23rd Street with all the important artists at the Hotel Chelsea.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and we knew them. And then we ended up in Greenwich Village on the best street, 10th Street--

speaker: interviewer
text: What street?

speaker: interviewee
text: --between Fifth and Sixth Avenue. Well, Lenny Bernstein lived across the hall-- Evans, the actor. And we became friendly with musicians and artists. And I guess we were born bohemians. And that was due to my mother, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were and are slightly bohemian, you know. but in our brains to live good lives, you know. [LAUGHS] Didn't marry an artist or-- I knew that I don't want to, you know? And [NON-ENGLISH] was married to a rich man. And she helped us tremendously.

speaker: interviewee
text: We still adore my brother-in-law. And [NON-ENGLISH] is like my mother, a goody-- good person. And she's always helped us with my child, with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about how you met your husband. You got to a child before we--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --have a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ended up-- the business went out of business on 57th Street. So I ended up on Seventh Avenue, which I didn't want to, because my brother said, you're not going to go out with anybody from Seventh Avenue. That's, you know, lower people than [LAUGHS] us, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh dear, garmento.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I got this job with Anne Klein. And Bob's brother was a salesman. His whole family, for three generations--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anne Klein is a designer for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anne Klein was a designer. I knew who she was. And my husband's family are in the Garment Center for three-- for three generations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his brother came and met me. And he says, boy, it would be nice to introduce you to my brother Bob. And he says, here is a telephone number. Call up. I said, Harold, I never call a man. They call me. [LAUGHS] So he called up on the telephone. And I spoke to Bob. And I felt something. I was engaged to Nathan's Hot Dogs son, but I didn't love him. And Bob's voice-- Nathan's [LAUGHS] Hot Dogs son-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm, and I didn't have one single penny to my name. Anyway, I met Bob on a blind date. And that was it. We fell in love. Something-- Bob-- we were drawn to each other because he's a very unusual man. He became my father and my mother. And he has had a very difficult life with me. But obviously, good enough if he-- if he stayed with me all these years. It's going to be 45 years next week--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mazel tov.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that we've been married. Thank you. But it's been difficult. I've been very sick. Especially after I became pregnant and I had my child, I had a total breakdown.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your child's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yolanda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you look very happy when you said her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, she is-- also, I feel that my mother is alive, you know? Because she, too, is a humanitarian and works with children who need her help, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is a PhD, clinical psychologist. And she is a therapist. And she just went into group therapy. And the hospital is very happy with her that she started this group therapy. And she didn't get married young. She was an artist first. And, you know, we let her do whatever she wanted to. And then one day she says, Mommy, I'm never going to make a living as an artist. So she went up to Columbia University. Needless to say, she was an A student. And they took her in this small program of 14 people. And she became a clinical psychologist. And she only married after that, which hurt me terribly. I felt maybe she'll never get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were concerned about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [SIGHS] And she always had problems, and she still has problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me-- you were saying something happened after you had Yolanda. Something happened to you. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I became terribly depressed. I couldn't take care of my child. I was hospitalized for a breakdown. Or you could call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nervous breakdown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nervous breakdown. Or you could call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Postpartum depression.

speaker: interviewee
text: Postpartum depression. And I was hospitalized for 10 months. And then I remained with that same psychiatrist that I met there. And there were no pills. There was nothing. But he saw me for another 10 years. And he saw me and my child. He made me just that strong. I always say, I brought up my child with the skin of my teeth because I was always sick. And my husband, thank God, is a healthy fellow. And he tried to be mother and father to my child. But when she saw me sitting depressed for months, she couldn't cope with it. We sent her to camp. And we did everything to try to make her life normal. But it couldn't be normal with a mother who wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were depressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was depressed a lot, an awful lot, and in treatment all the time, and spending a lot of money to keep me going. And we still do. 50 years later, and a lot of our money goes into medication and treatment. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that you, you have not been able to shake this, and your sisters have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age. I think that I was, you know, youngest. And I, I definitely believe in that, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: The trauma.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the trauma was much more distinct in me, even though I've been trying to deny it all these years, you know? It's inside of me. And the best psychiatrist, the most expensive psychiatrist, couldn't take my-- what I feel in my heart and make me well. But I have to be grateful that I am as well as I am. And my daughter, finally-- she didn't want to get married until she got her PhD. So she was engaged for a year, which upset me terribly. But I never said anything to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's Yolanda's husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he is a, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a pediatric endocrinologist. And he works in the same hospital in Cincinnati, the Children's Hospital, which is a very famous hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So something must have been going right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what everybody says. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to give yourself maybe a little credit?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little credit, yes, occasionally. But yes, she's doing-- she's helping people. She is the most wonderful mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so is David-- a wonderful father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have Hannah, who is five years old, brilliant and gorgeous. And there's Benjamin, who is out of this world. I never heard anybody so bright in my life-- and gorgeous. But I have trouble. I have trouble with Yolanda because I am still so involved in my hurt. It doesn't want to leave me. No matter how much therapy I had, for 45 years, it's there. And it's Auschwitz. And I'd love to get rid of it. But, I mean, I'm too old already. I have to learn and live with my, my-- I'm a crippled person, I feel of myself as such.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though in-between, you know, I, I designed clothes and I sold it on Madison Avenue, and I, I think I'm somewhat of a decorator, you know? I did beautiful things, and we traveled. And I must have given a lot of things to my husband because he wouldn't have stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I still feel like a victim, even though-- I mean, I have-- I always say this to people because they look at me. And I say, yes, I have everything in life-- a loving husband, children, grandchildren, my son-in-law. But I don't have [SOBBING] myself. [CRYING] [SOBBING] I don't. I'm not trying to impress what-- I've been left, I think, a little more crippled than some other people. This is my feeling about myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's a difficult life, even though I have everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now I'm getting older. You know what happens in life. Things become more exaggerated as you get older. And I'm-- I'd like to come to some terms, definitely, with my daughter, you know? That I-- I just couldn't tell her anything about Auschwitz when she was growing up. You never talked about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. [SOBBING] Never a word. And Bob didn't want to either. And then when she became a teenager, she says, Mommy. But there are ways [SOBBING] to tell a child in her own way. I said, but Yolanda, [SOBBING] I didn't have the capacity to do that. [SOBBING] And I've been feeling guilty about her. And I tried my best. And I think she's a wonderful, fabulous girl, and she's living up to my family. And that's very important to me, to-- especially to live up to my mother's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] humanity.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you feel she's as humane as your mother is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. She-- but she has more selfishness, thank God, more normal selfishness. She cares about her children. And she does things--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, your mother cared about you girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but she also cared about a lot of other people, which I didn't believe up until I was showed by this therapist that maybe your mother did also a disservice by being so good to everybody. It's hard for me to accept, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, your, your therapist is telling you, maybe your mother wasn't a saint?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, my God.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because human beings are not saints. But we were brought up with the idea. And I think I still feel that she was-- there's nobody in the world like my mother. There is just nobody. I'm very grateful that I had a mother [SOBBING] who taught me to be good to human beings. And I chose a husband who is-- feels the same way I do. And I brought up a child after all that suffering from the Germans. We remained totally human.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they're going back to killing people again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. There is a lot of anti-Semitism there in Germany. And I don't trust them, you know, the Germans. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I went back once. And I wouldn't go back again. I had to go back. Something drew me there. And it was very difficult. And I will never go back there again, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just the idea of the catharsis, you know, of seeing not many Jews in Budapest. Only my aunt was alive from a family of 500 people. And they will kill us just like the Germans. Not all of them, I suppose. But my experience was very bad with the Hungarians. And then worst is Auschwitz and the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Kisvarda, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I couldn't do that. Because I always felt-- [SOBBING] my mother loved yellow roses. She was what they call a Feinschmecker, you know? She loved beautiful--

speaker: interviewer
text: Refined.

speaker: interviewee
text: --things. Refined.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always have this dream [SOBBING] that I go and she has-- she's buried, but she isn't. And I'm going to her grave. And I put a yellow rose on there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this is not to happen. But my daughter and I, we send roses to each other, 12 beautiful roses on every birthday. And my husband brings me roses on all holidays and everything. [SOBBING] But I'm still a cripple. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You can't take Auschwitz out of a person, out of me. Maybe I was just born too sensitive. But I, I can't help that. I mean, I'm getting all the help, America's foremost psychiatrists and everything. And there is just a limit of how much they can help me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I should become stronger because my life is coming to a fruition, you know? And I try very hard. I really work with the therapist, with myself, with my husband. And now I will try to work more with Yolanda.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think with all that, I have to accept the fact that I will suffer all my life. Because if it didn't help for 50 years, it would be childish to think that I'll just get out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about the depression that you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: My depressions, yes, yes, yes. I'm doing better now. But I'm a very talented person. I was never--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have dreams? Do you have nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because-- not much, very little. Because I think that the, the medication kind of takes away dreaming, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: The medication that you're on now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any kind of-- when you hear sirens, when you see German shepherds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I hate them. I run away. And I call them Hund like they call their dog, you know. That's a Hund-- a German word, 'Hund,' a dog. I hate all dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it seems that I am so frightened that when the telephone rings, I go-- shake. When a siren or anything, or a car stops rapidly, I, I get all upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this like the loud noises you keep--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh I--

speaker: interviewer
text: --talking about in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yeah, it will never leave me, never. I have no control over it, absolutely none. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would like to feel better. And I'm going to work at it. But I don't know, because I have suffered so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little more about your grandchildren. Do you get to see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, about every three months we go over there. And Bob and I are very good with them. Although I don't do physically so much, but I am-- they love us--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because we are very good grandparents, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yolanda will-- you know, and creative grandparents, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're not boring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, not at all. We take Hannah out for a special luncheon when we're there. She's a little young lady. And Benjamin, I mean, he gets on the telephone. He's-- he was two years old. And he says-- he calls me Coco. And he says, Coco, you're pretty. Or I told him the other day-- he answers the telephone-- and always his father answers. So I said, hi, David. He says, I'm not David. I am Benjamin. And it's just amazing, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have an easier time with your grandchildren than you did with your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because I don't have all that responsibility. But it's not enough for me. I want to love unconditionally anything possible with my depressions, you know? I am a loving, giving human being. But these damn depressions just hold me back. And I don't want it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what choice do I have? To try to live as healthy as I can. But I'm not satisfied. I'm happy-- Your daughter, does she live in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they lived in Cincinnati. And so we fly up there. And, you know, she has a big household. And, you know, we just are the nice grandparents. And they would like to see us more. But it's very difficult for me to get myself together and go more often because of the problems I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about God before. You said how you told your father you didn't believe in him. How do you feel today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am getting much older. And I still haven't made any peace with God.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're angry.

speaker: interviewee
text: After Auschwitz, yes. There is no God for me. It's not a happy thought, you know? It would be nice to believe in something. But I believe still with all my heart and strength in humanity because I've met some wonderful people in this country who I only met because I came from a family where my mother was so special. And I guess I believe in humanity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever go to temple on the High Holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you fast on Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't do anything, no. I don't do anything. And that's what you say is the anger in me. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you light a candle for the day that your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about when your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not for my mother, no. I don't light candles. It's too painful. I couldn't look at a flickering life-- light because that's like life going out when you see a flickering light. I just-- I can't do it. I have a sense of beauty and something about life that I, [SIGHS] I don't want to face the ugliness. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Does your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --life--

speaker: interviewer
text: --husband go to temple on the High Holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: His family wasn't religious. He wasn't bar mitzvahed. He was-- came from a very good family. They're wonderful people. And they help me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: But yet you're living in a community, Great Neck, New York, in the United States that is probably-- maybe what, 70% Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even more?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they're not all religious, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you live among Jews. You feel more comfortable--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: --among Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I couldn't be more Jewish-- or Bob. Me and my daughter, very interesting-- my daughter and her husband, who liked a little hippiness in the '60s, they're conservative. They belong to a Reformed synagogue. She lights candles every Friday. And she goes with her children and her husband to the shul. And I am very happy about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see that.

speaker: interviewee
text: In spite [LAUGHS] of us--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see that.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know? They're searching for something. And it's good. I think it's healthy, and it's good. That I can't do it, I'm still denying something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe you believe in God. You're just very angry at him.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all possible. It's all possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you travel at all? Do you-- have you gone to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We've been-- South America. I became a Mayan student when we used to go 10 times in Mexico and take Yolanda with us everywhere. She's traveled around the world, you know. We've been in Egypt. We've been in China. We've been--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel when you went to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother was living there. And I was so proud of him, you know, doing his work for--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, he was head of PR at Ben-Gurion University. But he traveled all over the world to collect money for it-- not just the university, for all Israel causes, you know, and Jewish causes. And he has devoted his life. He's retired now. But his whole life-- he never was a big moneymaker. He made a living. But we're very proud of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He devoted his life to the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people, yes. And he's a beautiful human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter, the fact that she goes to temple and she lights candles makes you seem extraordinarily proud.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that's about?

speaker: interviewee
text: --it's, it's because I am really so Jewish. Even-- I'm trying to deny it, you know? I mean, I-- my heritage was beautiful. I can't live like that anymore. But my heart and soul is totally Jewish. And on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to Yad Vashem when you were in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I didn't go in. I waited outside. I never saw a movie. I-- if there is a Nazi appearing on television, Bob cuts it off because I can't take it. I can't take anything that has to do with the past. I am totally locked in. It's very difficult. But I can't come out of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you read the books by Elie Wiesel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He's a friend of my brother's and my sister's. And I-- no, I never would read anything about the Holocaust. Izabel said, well, why don't you come with me? Yesterday they had a meeting for Anne Frank. I said, I don't go anywhere. But I am going to appear today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was so difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much. You did a wonderful job. And we--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --appreciate it. And this will be going into archives-- historical archives all across the world. And your children and your grandchildren, your great grandchildren will see what happened. And anybody that ever wants to see what happened will see. And you did a wonderful job. And thank you so very much for letting us into your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. OK. And--
